CDH13 Transcription Is Directly Regulated by MYF5
Because CDH13 and FGFR4 were found to be enriched in
MYF5-tdTomato-positive hiPSC-MuPCs, we hypothesized
that MYF5 might control the expression of these genes.
Indeed, we found a strong correlation between MYF5 and
CDH13 expression and between MYF5 and FGFR4 expres-
sion in hiPSC-MuPCs (Figure 7A). To elucidate the possible
regulatory mechanism of MYF5 on CDH13 and FGFR4
expression, we investigated whether MYF5 overexpression
may induce CDH13 and/or FGFR4 expression. We trans-
fected
hiPSCs
(201B7)
with
a
doxycycline-inducible
mCherry-HA-MYF5 expression vector (Figure 7B) and
observed that most of the cells were mCherry positive, indi-
cating the efﬁcacy of the transfection (Figure 7C). More-
over, we conﬁrmed by qRT-PCR that MYF5 was efﬁciently
overexpressed in doxycycline-treated cells (Figure 7D).
Further, CDH13 expression was increased in MYF5-overex-
pressing undifferentiated hiPSCs, but FGFR4 expression
was unchanged (Figure 7E), suggesting that CDH13 gene
expression, but not FGFR4, is regulated by MYF5. Previ-
ously, Fgfr4 has been shown to be downstream of PAX3
in m

was unchanged (Figure 7E), suggesting that CDH13 gene

in mouse embryo myogenic progenitor cells (Lagha et al.,
2008) and in hiPSC-MuPCs (Magli et al., 2019), which
could explain why FGFR4 is expressed in hiPS-MuPCs.
To verify the MYF5 control over CDH13 expression, we
analyzed possible MYF5 binding motifs in the promoter
region of CDH13. We found putative binding sites in the
CDH13 promoter region, with both sequences containing
the E-box binding motif (Figure 7F) (Tierney et al., 2016).
To conﬁrm if MYF5 bound to these sites, we performed
chromatin immunoprecipitation (ChIP) for MYF5 fol-
lowed by qRT-PCR. qRT-PCR analysis revealed that DNA
fragments precipitated by the hemagglutinin (HA) anti-

site
sequences (Figure 7G), conﬁrming that MYF5 promotes
CDH13 expression by directly binding to the CDH13
promoter region at two different E-box sequences.

(Conerly et al., 2016). By using the available dat

DISCUSSION
By using MYF5 and PAX7 hiPSC reporter lines, we identi-
ﬁed two new markers for myogenic cell puriﬁcation:
CDH13 and FGFR4. Both markers could sort cells that
have high in vivo regeneration capacity and in vitro differen-
tiation potential. In addition, we showed that CDH13
expression is regulated by MYF5.
MYF5 is known to be the ﬁrst of the four myogenic regu-
latory factors (MYF5, MYOD1, MYOGENIN, and MRF4)
expressed during mammalian skeletal muscle development
(Ott et al., 1991). Notably, one study suggested that MYF5-
positive fetal MuPCs are the primary source of adult SCs,
but MYF5-positive embryonic MuPCs made a much smaller
contribution (Biressi et al., 2013). This ﬁnding is consistent
with late-stage MYF5-tdTomato-positive cells having higher
regenerative and therapeutic potential. On the other hand,
PAX7, which has been suggested to promote the progression
of somatic progenito

source: pmc/Characterization of hiPSC-Derived Muscle Progenitors Reveals Distinctive Markers.pdf
text:
ent
with late-stage MYF5-tdTomato-positive cells having higher
regenerative and therapeutic potential. On the other hand,
PAX7, which has been suggested to promote the progression
of somatic progenitors into the myogenic lineage and is
recognized as a marker of SCs (Seale et al., 2000), is also a
non-skeletal muscle-speciﬁc gene and known to be ex-
pressed in the developing central nervous system and neural
crest lineage cells (Buckingham and Relaix, 2007). Consis-
tently, we found that PAX7-Venus-positive cells were also
enriched for genes related to neuron development, suggest-
ing that in our culture system, PAX7-Venus-positive cells
might include neuronal lineage cells. Similarly, single-cell
RNA-seq analysis of PAX7-positive cells prepared from
different myogenic induction protocols indicated the
contamination of neuronal lineage cells in the sorted cell
populations (Xi et al., 2020).
Cell heterogeneity and non-myogenic lineage have been
described in several myogenic induction protocols (Xi
et al., 2020). Moreover, the transplantation of unsorted
hiPSC-MuPCs resulted in extremely poor regeneration
capacity (Hicks et al., 2018). Previously reported markers
for adult SCs, like N

source: pmc/Characterization of hiPSC-Derived Muscle Progenitors Reveals Distinctive Markers.pdf
text:
tocols (Xi
et al., 2020). Moreover, the transplantation of unsorted
hiPSC-MuPCs resulted in extremely poor regeneration
capacity (Hicks et al., 2018). Previously reported markers
for adult SCs, like NCAM1 and CD82 (Figures 6 and S5)
or ITGA7, MCAM, C-MET, CXCR4, and CD11b (Hicks
et al., 2018), have been shown to be unsuitable for purify-
ing myogenic cells, probably because the developmental
stage of hiPSC-MuPCs is rather fetal and different from
that of adult skeletal muscle tissues (Incitti et al., 2019; Xi
et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020).
We are not the only group to have reported surface
markers speciﬁc for hiPSC-MuPCs (Hicks et al., 2018; Magli
et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2018). Among them, the most used
are ERBB3 and NGFR (Hicks et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2020;
Sakai-Takemura et al., 2018), which were identiﬁed by
analyzing human fetal tissues. We studied these markers
in the Human Skeletal Muscle Atlas and found that they
are expressed not only by skeletal MuPCs but also by
Schwann cells across several myogenic induction methods
(Figures S5B and S5C). In our culture system, we conﬁrmed
the enriched gene expression of Schwann cell progenitor
cell markers on ERBB3-positive and NG

source: pmc/Characterization of hiPSC-Derived Muscle Progenitors Reveals Distinctive Markers.pdf
text:
cells across several myogenic induction methods
(Figures S5B and S5C). In our culture system, we conﬁrmed
the enriched gene expression of Schwann cell progenitor
cell markers on ERBB3-positive and NGFR-positive cells
(Figure S6C), suggesting other markers for higher puriﬁca-
tion are desired.
We identiﬁed FGFR4 and CDH13 by characterizing
hiPSC-MuPCs. FGFR4 and CDH13 have been consistently
shown to be expressed by quiescent SCs in human and
mouse based on RNA-seq experiments (De Micheli et al.,
2020a, 2020b; Pietrosemoli et al., 2017; Schaum et al.,
2018; Xi et al., 2020). Our results showed that the new
markers improved the efﬁcacy of hiPSC-MuPC puriﬁca-
tion, as indicated by the increased expression of MYF5
and PAX7 and improved in vitro differentiation across
different cell lines. Moreover, we conﬁrmed that 95% of
FGFR4-positive cells were PAX7-positive cells (Figures 6C
and 6D), indicating that FGFR4 can be used to highly purify
myogenic precursor cells expressing PAX7. Interestingly,
the expression of MYOD1 in FGFR4-positive cells was
lowest compared with cells positive for the other tested
markers. These ﬁndings were supported by our in vitro dif-
ferentiation and transplan

source: pmc/Characterization of hiPSC-Derived Muscle Progenitors Reveals Distinctive Markers.pdf
text:
ngly,
the expression of MYOD1 in FGFR4-positive cells was
lowest compared with cells positive for the other tested
markers. These ﬁndings were supported by our in vitro dif-
ferentiation and transplantation studies, indicating that
FGFR4 marks a subset of MuPCs with higher regeneration
capacity, while CDH13 marks most myogenic cells.
One of the advantages of using hiPSCs instead of embry-
onic stem cells is the possibility of transplanting autolo-
gous gene-corrected cells (Hallett et al., 2015; Hanna
et al., 2007; Shiba et al., 2016). By using CDH13 and
FGFR4 as markers, we puriﬁed myogenic cells from DMD
and DMD-corrected hiPSC-MuPCs. The DMD-corrected
cells were able to differentiate in vitro into myotubes ex-
pressing DYSTROPHIN, and after transplantation they
could restore DYSTROPHIN expression in engrafted myo-
tubes, strengthening the suitability of CDH13 and FGFR4
as markers for clinical applications. Moreover, some of
the transplanted cells were found to be PAX7 positive and
to be localized in the SC position, suggesting a possible
contribution to the SC pool. Further studies should address
the capacity of these cells to contribute to future rounds of
regeneration. Finally

source: pmc/Characterization of hiPSC-Derived Muscle Progenitors Reveals Distinctive Markers.pdf
text:
o be localized in the SC position, suggesting a possible
contribution to the SC pool. Further studies should address
the capacity of these cells to contribute to future rounds of
regeneration. Finally, in muscle transplanted with cells ex-
pressing either of the markers, we could identify hundreds
of h-SPECTRIN-positive tiny ﬁbers, which we speculate are
de novo ﬁbers that can fuse later to form new ﬁbers,
increasing the regeneration potential of the transplanted
cells.
In summary, this study identiﬁed two new cell-surface
markers for the puriﬁcation of hiPSC-MuPCs: CDH13,
which marks the majority of myogenic cells, and FGFR4,
which marks a subset of MuPCs with higher regeneration
capacity. We believe that these two markers will have
Stem Cell Reports j Vol. 16 j 883–898 j April 13, 2021
895


important implications for cell therapy for DMD and for
the study of basic muscle development.
EXPERIMENTAL PROCEDURES
Human iPSCs
The hiPSC lines 201B7 (Takahashi et al., 2007), Ff-WJ14s01, DMD,
and DMD-corrected were used for all the experiments. Ff-WJ14s01
(an HLA-homozygous iPSC line with the most frequent haplotype
in Japan, abbreviated as S01 in this article) was established from
cord b

text:
and DMD-corrected were used for all the experiments. Ff-WJ14s01
(an HLA-homozygous iPSC line with the most frequent haplotype
in Japan, abbreviated as S01 in this article) was established from
cord blood cells by using episomal vectors (Okita et al., 2013) at
the Facility for iPS Cell Therapy, CiRA, Kyoto University. S01 was
generated under written consent with approval of the Kyoto Uni-
versity Graduate School and Faculty of Medicine Ethics Committee
(approval nos. E1762, G567, and Rinsho71). The DMD iPSC line
(clone ID: CiRA00111) was prepared from dermal ﬁbroblasts of a
DMD patient with a deletion of exon 44 by using episomal vectors
(Li et al., 2015). The DMD-corrected iPSC line was generated by
knocking
in
exon
44
into
CiRA00111
(Li
et
al.,
2015).

of the Kyoto University Graduate School and Faculty of Medicine
Ethics Committee (approval nos. R0091 and G259).
201B7 and S01 were used to generate the PAX7-Venus and MYF5-
tdTomato reporter lines. All hiPSCs were cultured and maintained
under feeder-free culture conditions as previously described (Naka-
gawa et al., 2014). Brieﬂy, the cells were passaged once a week and
c

source: pmc/Characterization of hiPSC-Derived Muscle Progenitors Reveals Distinctive Markers.pdf
text:
Tomato reporter lines. All hiPSCs were cultured and maintained
under feeder-free culture conditions as previously described (Naka-
gawa et al., 2014). Brieﬂy, the cells were passaged once a week and
cultured on an iMatrix-511 (Nippi) pre-coated dish in StemFit
AK02N medium (Ajinomoto).
Statistical Analysis
All statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism
version 8.4.1 for Mac OS X (GraphPad Software, San Diego, CA,
USA). Differences between two groups were analyzed with two-
tailed Student’s t test, and differences between three or more
groups were analyzed by ANOVA with Tukey’s range test for multi-
ple comparisons. Differences were considered signiﬁcant when the
p value was <0.05. For the RNA-seq analysis, genes with at least a
2-fold enrichment and p < 0.05 were considered DEGs.

RESEARCH
Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic 
Potential and Mechanistic Insights from Clinical and Preclinical Studies
Ying Guo1 · Jianping Xie2 · Haiyun Luo1 · Yun Yuan1 · Fang Long1 · Jingyuan Meng1 · Hui Tan1 · Yuanping Li1 · 
Bibo Cai3 · Meiling Chen4 · Liyan Yang5 · Jingmei Zhong4 · Yanqing Zhu4 · Heng Shao6
Received: 24 June 2025 / Accepted: 11 December 2025 
© The Author(s) 2025
Abstract
Major depressive disorder (MDD) remains a leading cause of disability worldwide, and while selective serotonin reuptake 
inhibitors (SSRIs) are standard treatments, they have limited efficacy and adverse effects. Tetrahydrocurcumin (THC), a 
bioactive metabolite of curcumin, shows anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective properties that may augment antidepressant 
therapy. This study aimed to evaluate the efficacy and mechanisms of THC in treating MDD. A randomized, open-label, 
parallel-group pilot trial enrolled 19 patients with major depressive disorder (MDD) who received either escitalopram (ESC, 
10 mg/day) or ESC plus tetrahydrocurcumin (ESC + THC, 10

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
zed, open-label, 
parallel-group pilot trial enrolled 19 patients with major depressive disorder (MDD) who received either escitalopram (ESC, 
10 mg/day) or ESC plus tetrahydrocurcumin (ESC + THC, 10 mg ESC + 200 mg THC per day) for 29 days. Participants 
were randomized 1:1, with blinded raters assessing depressive severity using the 17-item Hamilton Depression Rating Scale 
(HAMD-17) at baseline and Day 29. Sixteen participants completed the primary endpoint assessment. Serum underwent 
data-independent acquisition (DIA-PASEF) proteomics, molecular docking, and ELISA. In parallel, chronic restraint stress 
(CRS) mice received THC (80 mg/kg) and were evaluated by behavior, immunofluorescence, and serum biomarkers. THC 
augmentation improved gastrointestinal symptoms in patients (p = 0.025), though total HAMD scores showed no significant 
group differences. Proteomic analysis identified 32 differentially expressed serum proteins, with THC modulating neuro-
degenerative pathways. In CRS mice, THC administration reversed anxiety- and depressive-like behaviors (open field, tail 
suspension, and forced swim tests), normalized prefrontal cortex (HSP90, KRT6A, P4HB, C1QA, APOM, CDH13) an

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
mice, THC administration reversed anxiety- and depressive-like behaviors (open field, tail 
suspension, and forced swim tests), normalized prefrontal cortex (HSP90, KRT6A, P4HB, C1QA, APOM, CDH13) and 
hippocampal (HSP90, P4HB, CDH13) protein expression in mice, and restored serum levels of P4HB, C1QA, and CDH13 
in both humans and mice, while additionally modulating LTF, TNF-α, IL-1β, cAMP, and DA in mice serum. These findings 
demonstrate that THC exerts multimodal antidepressant effects through coordinated anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective 
mechanisms. THC demonstrates antidepressant potential through anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective mechanisms, sup-
porting its use as a safe augmentation strategy in MDD treatment. Further trials are warranted to validate its clinical efficacy 
and molecular targets.
Keywords  Major depressive disorder · Tetrahydrocurcumin · Chronic restraint stress · Neuroinflammation · Proteomic 
profiling
Introduction
Depressive disorders affect approximately 3.4% of the global 
population, with Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) ris-
ing 16.4% from 2010–2019 [1]. In 2019, depression ranked 
among the top causes of Years Lived with Disability (Y

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
mately 3.4% of the global 
population, with Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) ris-
ing 16.4% from 2010–2019 [1]. In 2019, depression ranked 
among the top causes of Years Lived with Disability (YLD) 
worldwide [2]. Major depressive disorder (MDD) presents 
with psychological symptoms such as anhedonia, fatigue, and 
sleep disturbances[3] as well as physiological manifestations 
including agitation and gastrointestinal dysfunction [4] [5]. 
While selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are first-
line treatments, they often cause adverse effects (e.g., sexual 
dysfunction, weight gain, emotional blunting) [6]. Escitalo-
pram (ESC), though better tolerated than fluoxetine, may still 
induce hyponatremia [7], serotonin syndrome [8], or neuroen-
docrine disruptions [9]. To address these limitations, augmen-
tation strategies, combining SSRIs with adjunctive therapies, 
are increasingly used to enhance efficacy while reducing SSRI 
dosage and side effects.
Ying Guo, Jianping Xie, Haiyun Luo and Yun Yuan contributed 
equally to this work.

Tetrahydrocurcumin (THC), the primary metabolite of 
turmeric-derived curcumin, overcomes curcumin's low bio-
availability limitations while exhibiting enhanced absorption 
and stability [10, 11]. This natural compound demonstrates 
diverse pharmacological properties, including antioxidant 
[12], anti-inflammatory [13], and neuroprotective effects 
[14]. The compound's therapeutic potential for depres-
sion is particularly noteworthy given the established link 
between immune dysregulation and depression pathophysi-
ology. Elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, tumor necro-
sis factor-alpha (TNF-α), interleukin (IL)−1, and IL-6 in 
patients with depression may disrupt emotional regulation 
by crossing the blood–brain barrier [15], while depressive 
behaviors can further exacerbate inflammation [16]. Our 
previous research demonstrated THC's ability to down-
regulate inflammatory factors in the chronic restraint stress 
(CRS) depression model [17]. Importantly, THC shows an 
excellent

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
mation [16]. Our 
previous research demonstrated THC's ability to down-
regulate inflammatory factors in the chronic restraint stress 
(CRS) depression model [17]. Importantly, THC shows an 
excellent safety profile: acute toxicity studies revealed no 
adverse effects at doses up to 10,000 mg/kg [13], while sub-
chronic administration (400 mg/kg) caused no significant 
toxicological findings [18]. These properties position THC 
as a promising candidate for depression intervention, though 
clinical translation requires further investigation. Bridging 
the translational gap in THC treatment for MDD requires 
a bidirectional research approach. While preclinical stud-
ies reveal THC's molecular mechanisms, MDD's complex 
etiology-involving genetic, environmental, and psychosocial 
factors-demands clinical validation. A unidirectional para-
digm proves inadequate; instead, integrating clinical obser-
vations (to refine animal models) with preclinical investiga-
tions (to elucidate pathways) creates synergistic validation. 
This reciprocal strategy enhances the translational relevance 
of THC's antidepressant potential.
We hypothesized that THC's anti-inflammatory and neu-
roprotective p

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
creates synergistic validation. 
This reciprocal strategy enhances the translational relevance 
of THC's antidepressant potential.
We hypothesized that THC's anti-inflammatory and neu-
roprotective properties could safely enhance SSRI efficacy 
in depression treatment. To validate this, we designed a pio-
neering study with three key innovations: the first clinical 
trial of THC as an augmentation therapy in patients with 
depression; a bidirectional clinical-preclinical approach 
combining human data with CRS mouse model studies; and 
proteomic profiling to elucidate THC's molecular targets. 
This integrated strategy aims to establish THC's therapeutic 
potential while identifying novel targets for antidepressant 
development.
Methods and Materials
Study Design and Participants
This randomized, open-label, parallel-group pilot trial was 
conducted at the First People’s Hospital of Yunnan Prov-
ince between May and June 2023. A total of 19 patients 
with moderate-to-severe major depressive disorder (HAMD-
17 ≥ 17) were enrolled following psychiatric confirmation 
by two board-certified psychiatrists. Participants were ran-
domly assigned (1:1) to receive either escitalopram (ESC,

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
ssive disorder (HAMD-
17 ≥ 17) were enrolled following psychiatric confirmation 
by two board-certified psychiatrists. Participants were ran-
domly assigned (1:1) to receive either escitalopram (ESC, 
n = 9; 10 mg/day, Sichuan Kelun Pharmaceutical, China) 
or ESC + tetrahydrocurcumin (ESC + THC, n = 10; 10 mg 
ESC + 200 mg THC, Swanson, USA) for 29 days. HAMD 
testing was assessed at days 0 and 29. Three dropouts 
occurred: two (experimental) due to travel and one (control) 
refused final blood sampling, resulting in 16 participants 
who completed the full study protocol and were included in 
the final analysis. Detailed inclusion criteria and exclusion 
criteria in Supplementary File S1.
Proteomics Research
Protein Extraction and Digestion
Whole blood of patients collected at days 29 was centri-
fuged (1,500 g, 10 min) for serum isolation. Proteins were 
extracted using ice-cold lysis buffer (30 min), followed by 
centrifugation (12,000 g, 30 min, 4 °C). Protein concen-
tration was determined via BCA assay before SDS-PAGE 
separation. Aliquots (100 μg protein) were reduced (10 mM 
TCEP, 37 °C, 60 min), alkylated (40 mM IAA, RT, 40 min, 
dark), and precipitated (10,000 g, 20 min).

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
termined via BCA assay before SDS-PAGE 
separation. Aliquots (100 μg protein) were reduced (10 mM 
TCEP, 37 °C, 60 min), alkylated (40 mM IAA, RT, 40 min, 
dark), and precipitated (10,000 g, 20 min). Pellets were 
resuspended in 100 mM triethylammonium bicarbonate 
buffer, digested with trypsin (37 °C, overnight), then lyophi-
lized. Peptides were desalted (HLB columns), vacuum-dried, 
and quantified (Thermo Fisher peptide assay).
DIA Mass Spectrometry Detection
Peptides were reconstituted in MS loading buffer with 
iRT standards and analyzed using a timsTOF Pro2 system 
(Bruker, Germany) coupled to an EASY-nLC (Thermo, 
USA). Separation was performed on a C18 column 
(75 μm × 25 cm; Ionopticks, USA) with a 60-min gradient: 
3–28% B (0–45 min), 28–44% B (45–50 min), 44–90% B 
(50–55 min), and 90% B (55–60 min) (A: 2% ACN/0.1% 
FA; B: 80% ACN/0.1% FA; flow rate: 250 nL/min). DIA-
PASEF acquisition used positive ion mode (1.5 kV) with 
m/z 100–1700 scan range and 0.6–1.6 Vs·cm⁻2 ion mobility. 
Each TIMS cycle comprised 1 MS and 10 PASEF MS/MS 
scans across 64 DIA windows (25 isolations).
Protein Identification and Bioinformatics Analysis
Raw DIA data were processed in Spectronaut (v1

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
lity. 
Each TIMS cycle comprised 1 MS and 10 PASEF MS/MS 
scans across 64 DIA windows (25 isolations).
Protein Identification and Bioinformatics Analysis
Raw DIA data were processed in Spectronaut (v14) using 
the UniProt human database (https://​www.​unipr​ot.​org/​
taxon​omy/​9606) with iRT calibration. Quantification crite-
ria: ≥ 6 peptides/protein, ≥ 3 fragment ions/peptide (exclud-
ing shared/modified peptides). Differential expression 


Molecular Neurobiology          (2026) 63:323 	
Page 3 of 17 
  323 
(p < 0.05, fold-change > 1.2) was determined via t-test in R. 
Functional annotation utilized GO (http://​geneo​ntolo​gy.​org/) 
and KEGG pathway (http://​www.​genome.​jp/​kegg/) analy-
ses, with protein–protein interactions mapped via STRING 
v11.5. The data were analyzed through the free online plat-
form of majorbio cloud platform (cloud.majorbio.com).
Molecular Docking
The 3D structure of THC (SDF format) was obtained from 
PubChem (https://​pubch​em.​ncbi.​nlm.​nih.​gov/), while recep-
tor protein structures (PDB format) were downloaded from 
RCSB PDB (http://​www.​rcsb.​org). Using AutoDock, we pre-
pared the receptor by removing water molecules and adding 
hydrogens,

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
while recep-
tor protein structures (PDB format) were downloaded from 
RCSB PDB (http://​www.​rcsb.​org). Using AutoDock, we pre-
pared the receptor by removing water molecules and adding 
hydrogens, converted THC to PDB format using OpenBa-
bel (with added hydrogens), and performed docking with 
AutoGrid to generate 3D scoring grids covering active sites, 
followed by binding energy and hydrogen bond calculation 
with AutoDock. Results were visualized in PyMOL.
Behavioral Experiment in Mice
Thirty male ICR mice (6–8 weeks old, 20–22 g) were 
obtained from the Laboratory Animal Center of Kunming 
Medical University, China. The mice were housed in groups 
of five per cage under a 12 h light/dark cycle at 18–22 °C, 
with ad libitum access to food and water. After one week 
of acclimatization, they were randomly divided into three 
groups: control (CON), CRS, and CRS + THC (80 mg/kg). 
THC was procured from Yien Chemical Technology, Shang-
hai, China. All groups except CON underwent 21 days of 
CRS, which involved daily 4-h confinement in ventilated 
50 mL tubes. Intragastric administration was performed in 
all groups for 21 consecutive days prior to CRS induction 
[17].
Behavioral

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
ays of 
CRS, which involved daily 4-h confinement in ventilated 
50 mL tubes. Intragastric administration was performed in 
all groups for 21 consecutive days prior to CRS induction 
[17].
Behavioral tests: The open field test (OFT) was conducted 
in a 50 × 50 × 28 cm arena, with mouse activity recorded 
for 6 min. For the tail suspension test (TST), mice were 
suspended 2 cm from the tail tip, and immobility was quan-
tified over 6 min. In the forced swim test (FST), mice were 
placed in a water-filled cylinder (30 cm height × 12 cm diam-
eter; 20 cm depth, 25 ± 1 °C), and immobility duration was 
measured during the 6-min trial. All behavioral data were 
analyzed using the Smart Video Tracking Software V3.0 
(Panlab, Spain).
Immunofluorescence
After deep anesthesia and perfusion, mouse brains were 
extracted, paraffin-embedded, and sectioned coronally using 
a microtome. Forebrain sections were blocked with 10% goat 
serum (1 h), incubated (4 °C, overnight) with primary anti-
bodies, followed by Cy3-conjugated Goat Anti-Rabbit IgG 
(1 h). Following DAPI counterstaining (Solarbio, China), 
samples were imaged using an inverted fluorescence micro-
scope (Zeiss, Germany). Average op

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
followed by Cy3-conjugated Goat Anti-Rabbit IgG 
(1 h). Following DAPI counterstaining (Solarbio, China), 
samples were imaged using an inverted fluorescence micro-
scope (Zeiss, Germany). Average optical density of heat 
shock protein 90 (HSP90), keratin  6 A (KRT6A), protein 
disulfide-isomerase (P4HB), apolipoprotein M (APOM), 
cadherin-13 (CDH13), and complement component 1q 
subcomponent A (C1QA) was quantified in the prefrontal 
cortex and hippocampus (Antibody details in Supplementary 
Table 2).
Enzyme‑Linked Immunosorbent Assay Analysis
Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay Analysis (ELISA) 
kits were used to measure the levels of P4HB, CDH13, 
C1QA, Lactotransferrin (LTF), TNF-α, IL-1β, cyclic adeno-
sine monophosphate (cAMP), dopamine (DA), 5-hydroxy-
tryptamine (5-HT), and mannan-binding lectin serine pro-
tease 2 (MASP2) in serum of patients or mice. All ELISA 
kits were used according to the manufacturer's instructions.
Statistical Analysis
Demographic and clinical characteristics were analyzed 
using independent samples t-tests (continuous variables) 
and chi-square tests (categorical variables). HAMD-17 
symptom improvement was assessed by change scores 
(Δ = Post–Pre),

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
stics were analyzed 
using independent samples t-tests (continuous variables) 
and chi-square tests (categorical variables). HAMD-17 
symptom improvement was assessed by change scores 
(Δ = Post–Pre), with between-group differences evaluated 
using Mann–Whitney U tests (p < 0.05) due to non-normal 
distribution. For human and mouse studies (behavioral, 
immunofluorescence, and serological data), normality test-
ing was first performed. Multiple group comparisons used 
one-way ANOVA with LSD post hoc tests, while two-group 
comparisons employed either t-tests (normal distribution) 
or Mann–Whitney U tests (non-normal), with significance 
set at p < 0.05. All analyses were conducted in SPSS 19.0.
Results
Effects of THC on HAMD‑17
The study found no significant differences in baseline 
characteristics (age, sex, HAMD-17 total scores) or post-
treatment total scores between ESC and ESC + THC groups 
(all p > 0.05, Table 1). However, itemized analysis revealed 
that THC augmentation specifically improved gastrointes-
tinal symptoms (U = 16.00, p = 0.025) compared to ESC 
monotherapy, while showing marginally significant lesser 
improvement in psychomotor agitation (p = 0.053). No 
betwe

monotherapy, while showing marginally significant lesser 
improvement in psychomotor agitation (p = 0.053). No 
between-group differences were observed in core depressive 
symptoms (low mood, guilt, suicidal ideation) or anxiety 
measures (Table 2). These data suggest THC may selec-
tively ameliorate certain somatic symptoms when combined

Proteomic analysis of THC on Patients 
with Depression
In the PCA plot (Fig. 1B), samples clustered by treatment 
group, demonstrating group-specific proteomic signa-
tures. Pearson correlation heatmap analysis demonstrated 
high intra-group reproducibility among biological rep-
licates (Fig. 1C). Comparative analysis identified 32 sig-
nificantly altered proteins (22 upregulated, 10 downregu-
lated) in ESC + THC versus ESC groups (Fig. 1D). These 
proteins were assigned by GO enrichment to several key 
functional terms, such as nucleotide binding, nucleoside 
phosphate binding, and programmed cell death, among oth-
ers (Fig

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
ps (Fig. 1D). These 
proteins were assigned by GO enrichment to several key 
functional terms, such as nucleotide binding, nucleoside 
phosphate binding, and programmed cell death, among oth-
ers (Fig. 1E). KEGG enrichment highlighted involvement 
in neurodegeneration pathways (Parkinson's, Alzheimer's), 
HIF-1 signaling, and glycolysis (Fig. 1F). STRING net-
work analysis (12 nodes, 3 edges) revealed protein–protein 
interactions among differentially expressed proteins in LTF, 
HSP90AA1, KRT6A, P4HB, MASP2, APOM, and CDH13 
(Fig. 1G). These results confirm that THC augmentation 
induces distinct serum proteome alterations and engages 
different molecular targets compared to escitalopram mono-
therapy in patients with MDD.

Molecular Docking Analysis between THC 
and the Core Target Proteins
To further elucidate the molecular interactions between 
THC and key target proteins identified through proteomic 
analysis, molecular docking simulations were performed. 
As illustrated in Fig. 2, THC fits well into the binding 
pockets of the core targets, forming three hydrogen bonds 
with APOM, two hydrogen bonds with LTF, P4HB, and 
C1QA, and one hydrogen bond with HSP90AA1, MASP, 
KRT6A, and CDH13. THC exhibited varying bind-
ing affinities to these proteins, with binding energies of 
−5.79  kcal/mol (HSP90AA1), −5.24  kcal/mol (LTF), 
−3.61 kcal/mol (P4HB), and −3.06 kcal/mol (MASP), 
−5.29  kcal/mol (APOM), −1.26  kcal/mol (KRT6A), 
−4.80 kcal/mol (CDH13), and −5.70 kcal/mol (C1QA). 
The binding energies were converted to predicted dis-
sociation constants (Kd) using the relationship ΔG = RT 
ln(Kd): HSP90AA1: Kd ≈ 40 µM (ΔG = −5.79 kcal/mol); 
C1QA: Kd ≈ 47 µM (ΔG = −5.70 kcal/mol); APOM: Kd 
≈ 195 µM (ΔG = −5.29 kcal/mol); LTF: Kd ≈ 286 µM 
(ΔG = −5.24 kcal/mol). Molecular docking results indicated 
that although THC

C1QA: Kd ≈ 47 µM (ΔG = −5.70 kcal/mol); APOM: Kd 
≈ 195 µM (ΔG = −5.29 kcal/mol); LTF: Kd ≈ 286 µM 
(ΔG = −5.24 kcal/mol). Molecular docking results indicated 
that although THC binds to these proteins with affinities in

commonly observed for interactions between many natural

Anxiolytic‑ and Antidepressant‑like Effects of THC 
on CRS Mice
To evaluate the anxiolytic- and antidepressant-like effects of 
THC, mice were subjected to the CRS model (Fig. 3A). In 
the first and second weeks, CRS group showed weight loss 
compared to CON group (Fig. 3B). The locomotor activity 
trajectory in the OFT was displayed (Fig. 3C). No intergroup 
differences were observed in total distance of OFT (Fig. 3D), 
indicating that THC did not affect the motor function of 
mice. CRS-induced mice showed reduced center distance 
(p < 0.05), center time (p < 0.05), zone transitions (p < 0.01), 
and rearing number (p < 0.01) in OFT, along with increased 
immobility time of TST (p < 0.05) and FST (p < 0.05) ver-
sus CON group. THC treatment effectively reversed these 
CRS-induced behavioral deficits, si

text:
g number (p < 0.01) in OFT, along with increased 
immobility time of TST (p < 0.05) and FST (p < 0.05) ver-
sus CON group. THC treatment effectively reversed these 
CRS-induced behavioral deficits, significantly improving 
center distance (p < 0.05), center time (p < 0.05), zone tran-
sitions (p < 0.05), rearing number (p < 0.05), and reducing 
immobility time of TST (p < 0.01) and FST (p < 0.05) com-
pared to CRS group (Fig. 3E-J). These observations col-
lectively suggest THC's anxiolytic- and antidepressant-like 
effects for CRS.

Discussion
In this study, the CRS model was used to induce chronic 
stress through repeated restraint, a well-validated approach 
for modeling depression-like behavior and HPA axis dys-
regulation. This paradigm offers high standardization, repro-
ducibility, and low mortality, and reliably produces anxiety-/
depressive-like phenotypes along with HPA axis and inflam-
matory changes. The behavioral outcomes (OFT, TST, FST) 
and cytokine shifts (TNF-α, IL-1β) are well-established and 
closely match the endpoints examined in our work. CRS was 
selected because it effectively mimics core chronic stress-
induced symptoms-inflammatory response and behavioral 
deficits, thereby allowing as

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
sely match the endpoints examined in our work. CRS was 
selected because it effectively mimics core chronic stress-
induced symptoms-inflammatory response and behavioral 
deficits, thereby allowing assessment of drug efficacy with-
out confounding influences from other disorder-specific 
symptoms. Additionally, the model accommodates repeated 
oral gavage necessary for THC administration. Notably, the 
observation that paroxetine ameliorates behavioral deficits 
in the stress re-stress model aligns with our finding that anti-
depressant treatment significantly improves CRS-induced 
behavioral abnormalities [19]. This consistency supports the 
validity of both models in revealing mechanisms underlying 
antidepressant drug actions.
Our findings demonstrate that THC exerts anti‑inflam-
matory effects in a CRS‑induced depression model, as indi-
cated by reduced serum levels of IL‑1β and TNF‑α. This 
anti‑inflammatory activity may contribute to the alleviation 
of depression‑related behaviors, particularly by ameliorating 
inflammation‑associated gastrointestinal symptoms [5]. At 
the neurochemical level, THC restored stress‑induced DA 
depletion and 5‑HT levels, indicating a synergisti

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
rs, particularly by ameliorating 
inflammation‑associated gastrointestinal symptoms [5]. At 
the neurochemical level, THC restored stress‑induced DA 
depletion and 5‑HT levels, indicating a synergistic modula-
tory influence on monoaminergic systems. The preservation 
of 5‑HT in CRS mice further suggests that early stress may 
predominantly impair dopaminergic pathways. In a myo-
cardial infarction‑induced depression model, Ginkgolide 
B lowered IL‑1β via the STAT3 pathway and raised 5‑HT, 
supporting the concept that anti‑inflammatory interventions 
can normalize neurotransmitter imbalances [20]. Other com-
pounds, including 6‑Shogaol, hibiscetin, and malvidin, also 
alleviate depression‑ and motor‑related deficits by lower-
ing pro‑inflammatory cytokines and modulating DA, 5‑HT, 
and GABA [21–23]. The cAMP signaling pathway appears 
central to integrating inflammatory and neurotransmitter 
responses. Corticosterone‑induced depression‑like behaviors 
are associated with hippocampal apoptosis and suppressed 
cAMP expression [24], consistent with reports of impaired 
cAMP signaling in chronic stress [25]. Weakened cAMP/
PKA signaling attenuates CREB phosphorylation, com-
promising t

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
optosis and suppressed 
cAMP expression [24], consistent with reports of impaired 
cAMP signaling in chronic stress [25]. Weakened cAMP/
PKA signaling attenuates CREB phosphorylation, com-
promising the transcription of neurotrophic factors such as 
BDNF and leading to synaptic dysfunction and neuronal 
loss-key processes in depression pathogenesis. 5‑HT₆ recep-
tor activation alleviates neuropathic pain‑related antidepres-
sant via the AC‑cAMP‑PKA‑MEK‑CREB‑BDNF cascade 
[26], and oxytocin protects dopaminergic function against 
corticosterone through PKA/CREB signaling [27]. Nota-
bly, the anti‑inflammatory and neurotransmitter‑modulating 
effects of THC may converge on the cAMP/PKA/CREB/
BDNF pathway. Collectively, these mechanisms form a posi-
tive feedback cycle: reducing inflammation helps restore 
neurotransmitter balance, which in turn further dampens 
neuroinflammatory responses.
Our research results show that THC reverses the elevated 
HSP90 in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus of depres-
sive-like mice. Emerging evidence demonstrates that HSP90 
plays a central role in the integrated immune-neuroplasti-
city-stress pathway of major depressive disorder. HSP90 
cooperat

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
ampus of depres-
sive-like mice. Emerging evidence demonstrates that HSP90 
plays a central role in the integrated immune-neuroplasti-
city-stress pathway of major depressive disorder. HSP90 
cooperates with its co-chaperone FKBP51 to regulate glu-
cocorticoid receptor function, jointly maintaining HPA axis 
stress response homeostasis [28]; dysregulation of this pro-
cess leads to glucocorticoid receptor resistance, HPA axis 
dysfunction, and neuroinflammation [29]. Preclinical studies 
confirm that targeting HSP90 effectively ameliorates depres-
sive-like behaviors: quercetin inhibits HSP90 to suppress 
NLRP3 inflammasome activation and levels of cytokines 
(IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α), alleviating hippocampal neuro-
inflammation [30]; while rosmarinic acid downregulates 
HSP90 expression while activating the BDNF/TrkB/PI3K 
signaling pathway to promote neurogenesis [31]. Further-
more, HSP90 inhibition has been found to possess neuropro-
tective potential by preserving blood–brain barrier integrity 
[32]. Uncaria rhynchophylla, which exhibits neuroprotective 
effects both in vivo and in vitro, can increase the levels of 
dopamine and its metabolites by inhibiting the expression 
of

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
er integrity 
[32]. Uncaria rhynchophylla, which exhibits neuroprotective 
effects both in vivo and in vitro, can increase the levels of 
dopamine and its metabolites by inhibiting the expression 
of HSP90 [33]. These findings collectively establish that the 
anti-inflammatory and neurotransmitter-modulating effects 
of THC may be linked to HSP90-mediated immune regula-
tion of the stress response and its control of neuroplasticity.
Our findings demonstrate that THC administration sig-
nificantly reduced P4HB levels in the serum of patients 
with MDD compared to those receiving escitalopram 
monotherapy. This effect was paralleled by a downregula-
tion of P4HB both systemically (in serum) and in key brain

regions (the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus) in the CRS 
mouse model of depression. As an endoplasmic reticulum 
(ER)-resident chaperone and oxidoreductase, P4HB plays 
an essential role in protein folding and disulfide bond for-
mation. Its dysfunction under nitrosative stress leads to 
impaired protein folding, ER stress, and neuronal apopto-
sis [34]. In the context of MDD, P4HB dysregulation may 
amplify ER stress and promote apoptotic signaling, thereby 
contributing to the disruption of neuro-immune and neuro-
plasticity pathways. Notably, ovariectomized rats exhibiting 
depression-like behaviors show elevated P4HB expression, 
further linking this

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
contributing to the disruption of neuro-immune and neuro-
plasticity pathways. Notably, ovariectomized rats exhibiting 
depression-like behaviors show elevated P4HB expression, 
further linking this protein to stress-related pathophysiol-
ogy [35]. Moreover, the antidepressant paroxetine has been 
reported to modulate P4HB expression in neural cells [36], 
suggesting a role for P4HB in the mechanism of antidepres-
sant action. By decreasing ROS production and inactivating 
the NF-κB pathway, P4HB knockout inhibits the inflamma-
tory responses of macrophages, including migration, M1 
polarization, and secretion of TNF-α and IL-6 [37]. DA can 
bind to P4HB, and the protective effect of DA against oxida-
tive neurodegeneration is attributed to its inhibition of P4HB 
[38]. Our results imply that THC’s antidepressant effects 
may involve the inhibition of P4HB, thereby attenuating 
neuroinflammatory responses and promoting DA.
We report that THC administration upregulates CDH13 
levels in both patients with MDD and mouse models 
of depression. CDH13, a glycosylphosphatidylinositol-
anchored member of the cadherin superfamily, has been 
identified as a risk gene for depression and seve

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
both patients with MDD and mouse models 
of depression. CDH13, a glycosylphosphatidylinositol-
anchored member of the cadherin superfamily, has been 
identified as a risk gene for depression and several other 
neuropsychiatric conditions, including bipolar disorder, 
schizophrenia, ADHD, and autism spectrum disorders [39, 
40]. Functionally, CDH13 is implicated in axonal growth, 
synapse formation, and neuronal plasticity-processes fre-
quently impaired in depression [41]. Human genetic stud-
ies further associate CDH13 variants with personality traits 
such as low adaptability and altered neural responses during 
cognitive tasks, particularly in ADHD, underscoring its role 
in stress adaptation and executive function [42]. In Cdh13-
deficient mice, disrupted excitatory-inhibitory balance in 
hippocampal circuits, deficits in learning and memory, and 
maladaptive behavioral responses to early-life stress have 
been observed, supporting CDH13’s function as a neuro-
protective factor and a mediator of synaptic plasticity [43]. 
Specific knockout of CDH13 in 5-HT neurons increases 
neuronal density in the dorsal raphe and prefrontal cortex 
innervation, leading to delayed learning an

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
d a mediator of synaptic plasticity [43]. 
Specific knockout of CDH13 in 5-HT neurons increases 
neuronal density in the dorsal raphe and prefrontal cortex 
innervation, leading to delayed learning and impulsive-like 
behavior in mice [44]. CDH13 gene polymorphisms have 
higher C-reactive protein level and are significantly associ-
ated with immune-mediated and inflammatory diseases[45]. 
Importantly, CDH13 expression is regulated by chronic 
stress and participates in modulating neural and behavio-
ral responses to environmental challenges. By upregulating 
CDH13 expression, THC may promote the restoration of 
synaptic integrity and adaptive plasticity, thereby counter-
acting the detrimental effects of chronic stress and systemic 
inflammation on neural circuit function.
Our study found that THC reduces serum C1QA lev-
els in patients with depression, and this phenomenon was 
consistently observed both peripherally (in serum) and 
centrally (in the prefrontal cortex) in mouse models of 
depression. The complement system, as a key component 
of innate immunity, participates in neuroimmune regula-
tion through multiple activation pathways. Among these, 
C1QA, the initiating protein

of 
depression. The complement system, as a key component 
of innate immunity, participates in neuroimmune regula-
tion through multiple activation pathways. Among these, 
C1QA, the initiating protein of the classical pathway, plays 
a critical role in neurodegenerative and psychiatric disor-
ders. Chronic stress can activate the complement system, 
promote neuroinflammation, and exacerbate depression-
like behaviors [46]. Clinical studies have also shown that 
serum C1Q levels are significantly elevated in patients with 
depression and are positively correlated with age [47]. In 
aged rat models exhibiting anxiety-like behaviors, C1QA 
gene expression is similarly upregulated [48]. Furthermore, 
elevated C1QA levels have been observed in patients with 
long COVID experiencing gastrointestinal symptoms and 
cognitive impairment [49]. Notably, in our study, while THC 
ameliorated depressive- and anxiety-like behaviors, it not 
only effectively alleviated gastrointestinal symptoms but also 
significantly reduced C1QA expression. In an Alzheimer's 
disease mouse model, upregulation of the immune system 
is evidenced by increased expression of proinflammatory 
genes, including elevated

so 
significantly reduced C1QA expression. In an Alzheimer's 
disease mouse model, upregulation of the immune system 
is evidenced by increased expression of proinflammatory 
genes, including elevated levels of C1QA and the cytokines 
TNF-α and IL-18. The rise in C1QA triggers excessive 
microglial activation, which in turn drives aberrant synap-
tic pruning, ultimately leading to synapse loss and disrupted 
synaptic transmission [50]. This suggests that by reducing 
C1QA levels, THC may attenuate microglia-mediated exces-
sive synaptic pruning, restore synaptic integrity and adaptive 
plasticity, and thereby counteract the detrimental effects of 
stress and systemic inflammation on neural circuit function.

Similarly, keratin proteins including KRT6A have emerged 
as potential biomarkers in MDD [52]. KRT6A is overex-
pressed in AD patients and can be further stimulated by 
proinflammatory cytokines, suggesting its involvement in 
inflammatory skin pathology. Mechanistically, KRT6A 
activates STAT3 and enhances proinflammatory cytokine 
expression in keratinocytes by reducing JAK1 ubiquitina-
tion [53]. Furthermore, it also functions as a negative reg-
ulator of IL-17a expression, thereby blocking IL-17 sign-
aling [54]. Collectively, these findings position KRT6A as 
a multi-faceted regulator of skin inflammation. Its down-
regulation following THC treatment may thus indicate a 
broader role in immune modulation. Concurrently, THC-
induced APOM upregulation may activate dual protective 
mechanisms. APOM, by binding sphingosine-1-phosphate 
(S1P), not only triggers anti-inflammatory signaling via 
S1P receptors but also suppresses inflammation by inhib-
iting NF-κB nuclear translocation throu

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
ms. APOM, by binding sphingosine-1-phosphate 
(S1P), not only triggers anti-inflammatory signaling via 
S1P receptors but also suppresses inflammation by inhib-
iting NF-κB nuclear translocation through the PI3K/
AKT pathway [55, 56]. This anti-inflammatory role is 
further corroborated by evidence showing that APOM-
S1P inhibits ox-LDL-induced inflammation in HUVECs 
via the S1PR2-mediated PI3K/Akt pathway[57] and that 
APOM⁻/⁻ mice exhibit significant increases in pro-inflam-
matory proteins like iNOS, NF-κB, and IL-1β [58]. Col-
lectively, by rebalancing LTF, KRT6A, and APOM, THC 
may mitigate stress-induced inflammation, ultimately alle-
viating depressive symptomatology.
Our integrated approach demonstrates strong concord-
ance between computational predictions and experimental 
validation. Molecular docking identified several protein 
targets of THC, including HSP90AA1 and C1QA, whose 
significant binding affinities were corroborated by their 
altered expression in proteomic, immunofluorescence, and/
or serum analyses. Other targets such as KRT6A, P4HB, 
APOM, and CDH13 were also consistently dysregulated 
across multiple assays. This docking-guided framework 
provided mechan

text:
unofluorescence, and/
or serum analyses. Other targets such as KRT6A, P4HB, 
APOM, and CDH13 were also consistently dysregulated 
across multiple assays. This docking-guided framework 
provided mechanistic hypotheses for THC’s pleiotropic 
effects and prioritized key targets for translational develop-
ment. Notably, proteins consistently altered in both human 
serum and mouse brain (P4HB, C1QA, CDH13) form a 
High-Confidence Translational Core, suggesting their 
central role in THC’s therapeutic mechanism beyond spe-
cies- or compartment-specific noise. Discordant biomark-
ers offer additional insights: LTF (serum-specific) may 
reflect a peripherally-driven immune response, whereas 
targets like APOM and HSP90AA1 (brain-specific) likely 
indicate central nervous system-specific mechanisms not 
fully captured in peripheral circulation. Collectively, this 
cross-species signature provides a systems-level roadmap 
of THC’s action and informs future biomarker-driven stud-
ies in depression.
In addition, THC lacks the α,β-unsaturated diene struc-
ture and consequently exhibits improved solubility, physico-
chemical stability, and bioavailability compared with cur-
cumin [13, 59]. Precl

In addition, THC lacks the α,β-unsaturated diene struc-
ture and consequently exhibits improved solubility, physico-
chemical stability, and bioavailability compared with cur-
cumin [13, 59]. Preclinical studies have shown that chronic 
oral administration of THC results in higher hepatic and 
serum concentrations of THC and its glucuronide or sulfate 
conjugates relative to curcumin, indicating superior gas-
trointestinal absorption [60]. According to the toxicologi-
cal evaluation by the European Food Safety Authority, the 
recommended maximum daily intake for adults (excluding 
pregnant and lactating women) is 300 mg when used as a 
dietary supplement, with no observed adverse nutritional or 
genotoxic effects [61]. This dosage provides a reference for 
the safety range in future clinical studies. To enhance the 
oral absorption of curcumin and its derivatives, a variety 
of formulation strategies have been developed [62]. Cur-
rent delivery platforms, such as micelles, liposomes, phos-
pholipid complexes, micro- and nano emulsions, solid lipid 
nanoparticles, nanostructured lipid carriers, biopolymer 
nanoparticles, and microgels, have been shown to markedly 
improve bioavai

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
, phos-
pholipid complexes, micro- and nano emulsions, solid lipid 
nanoparticles, nanostructured lipid carriers, biopolymer 
nanoparticles, and microgels, have been shown to markedly 
improve bioavailability [63]. Based on previous population 
and longitudinal proteomic studies, body-fluid protein pro-
files have been shown to be systematically associated with 
depressive phenotypes and treatment outcomes [64]. Future 
research may employ individualized proteomic assessments 
to monitor dynamic changes in differential proteins during 
THC treatment, thereby elucidating its potential regulatory 
mechanisms and biomarker value in antidepressant response.
Limitation
This study has several limitations. We planned a 1:1 allo-
cation of 20 participants; however, 19 were randomized 
and 3 discontinued, which lowered power for the primary 
endpoint and may increase the chance of a type II error. 
Psychiatrists performing the ratings were blinded, yet the 
open-label design with active antidepressant therapy in both 
groups may have introduced expectancy and reporting bias. 
The lack of a between-group difference in HAMD-17 total 
should therefore be viewed cautiously. The combination of

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
ressant therapy in both 
groups may have introduced expectancy and reporting bias. 
The lack of a between-group difference in HAMD-17 total 
should therefore be viewed cautiously. The combination of 
gastrointestinal improvement and concordant proteomic and 
inflammatory signals indicates that THC may preferentially 
affect somatic and inflammatory domains during the first 
four weeks. Longer follow-up is needed to evaluate other 
HAMD subdomains. Finally, this was a single-center pilot 
with a 29-day treatment course, which limits generalizabil-
ity and may miss delayed mood responses. Larger, blinded, 
multicenter trials with extended follow-up are needed.

Conclusion
This study provides the first clinical and mechanistic evi-
dence supporting THC as a novel augmentation strategy 
for depression. In clinical trials, THC improved gastro-
intestinal symptoms in patients with depression, while 
proteomic profiling revealed its unique capacity to mod-
ulate 32 serum proteins. Preclinically, THC reversed 
CRS-induced behavioral deficits in mice, ameliorating 
anxiety- and depression-like behaviors and restoring 
pr

source: pmc/Tetrahydrocurcumin for Major Depressive Disorder with Therapeutic Potential and .pdf
text:
vealed its unique capacity to mod-
ulate 32 serum proteins. Preclinically, THC reversed 
CRS-induced behavioral deficits in mice, ameliorating 
anxiety- and depression-like behaviors and restoring 
prefrontal-hippocampal protein. Crucially, THC exerts 
multimodal antidepressant effects through coordinated 
anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective mechanisms. These 
findings establish a mechanistic basis for THC's potential 
as an effective augmentation strategy.
